elements.sent-2411-001: We demonstrate that $n$-dimension closed Einstein manifolds, whose smallest eigenvalue of the curvature operator of the second kind of $\mathring{R}$ satisfies $\lambda_1 \ge -\theta(n) \bar\lambda$, are either flat or round spheres, where $\bar \lambda$ is the average of the eigenvalues of $\mathring{R}$, and $\theta(n)$ is defined as in equation \eqref{1.2 .}
elements.sent-2411-002: Our result improves a celebrated result (Theorem \ref{thmp ) concerning Einstein manifolds with nonnegative curvature operator of the second kind.}
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we explore the sphere theorems for Einstein manifolds within the context of conditions imposed by the curvature operator of the second kind.
elements.sent-2411-004: The study of the curvature operator of the second kind, as defined in Section \ref{sec 2.2 below, can be traced back to a conjecture by Nishikawa \cite{Nishikawa86 , which asserts that a closed Riemannian manifold with positive (or nonnegative) curvature operator of the second kind is diffeomorphic to a spherical space form (or a Riemannian locally symmetric space).}}
elements.sent-2411-005: Recently, Cao-Gursky-Tran \cite{CGT23 and Li \cite{Li21 have confirmed Nishikawa’s conjecture for the positive and nonnegative cases, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-006: For further information and recent developments on this topic, we direct readers to the following references:\cite{Li22Kahler,Li22product,Li22PAMS,Li24,FL24,NPW22,NPWW22, DF24, DFY24 .}
elements.sent-2411-007: For Einstein manifolds, a well-known result in this aspect is the following theorem.
elements.sent-2411-008: A closed Einstein manifold with nonnegative curvature operator of the second kind is either flat or a round sphere.
elements.sent-2411-009: There is a rich literature on Theorem \ref{thmp .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Kashiwada \cite{Kashiwada93 proved that compact manifold $M$ with harmonic curvature and positive curvature operator of the second kind is a space of constant curvature.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Cao, Gursky and Tran \cite{CGT23 demonstrated that compact Einstein manifolds with 4-positive and 4-nonnegative curvature operator of the second kind are constant curvature spaces and locally symmetric spaces, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Subsequently, Li \cite{Li22JGA relaxed the assumption to $4\frac{1 {2}$-positive (respectively, nonnegative) curvature operator of the second kind.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Nienhaus, Petersen and Wink \cite{NPW22 showed that $n$ dimensional compact Einstein manifolds with $ N'$-nonnegative ($ N'<\frac{3n(n+2) {2(n+4)}$) curvature operators of the second kind are either rational homology spheres or ﬂat.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Recently, Dai, Fu and Yang \cite{DFY24 showed that a compact Einstein manifold of dimension $n\ge 4$ with nonnegative curvature operator of the second kind is flat or a space form of positive curvature, see also \cite{DF24 .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Cao-Gursky-Tran \cite{CGT23 , Nienhaus-Petersen-Wink \cite{NPW22 , Li \cite{Li21 and Dai-Fu \cite{DF24 have proved many excellent results under various curvature conditions.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Recently, Li \cite{Li24 introduced new lower bound conditions on the curvature operator of the second kind (see \cite[Definition 1.2]{Li24 ) and proved optimal sphere theorems.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Based on this framework, we focus on Li's condition with $\a=1$, namely \eqref{1.1 , which is weaker than nonnegative curvature operator of the second kind.}
elements.sent-2411-018: To state our result, we introduce some notations: $(M^n, g)$ is an $n$ dimensional Riemannian manifold, $\mathring{R}$ represents the curvature operator of the second kind, $N=(n+2)(n-1)/2$ is the dimension of $S_0^2(T_p M)$, $\l_1\le \l_2\le \cdots\le \l_N$ are the eigenvalues of $\mathring{R}$, and $\bar{\l =\sum_{j=1 ^N \l_j/N$ is the average of $\l_j$.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: The main result of this paper is as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-020: Let $(M^n,g)$ be a closed Einstein manifold of dimension $n \ge 4$.. If \begin{equation}\label{1.1} \l_1\ge -\theta(n) \bar{\l}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-021: Here $\theta(n)$ is a positive constant given by \begin{equation}\label{1.2}  \theta(n)=\frac{3(N-1)(N+1-n)}{(N-1)(N-3)+3n(N-2)}-1. \end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-022: Theorem \ref{thm1 essentially represents a gap theorem for Einstein manifolds, in the sense that if the curvature operator of the second kind satisfies \eqref{1.1 , then the curvature operator of the second kind is nonnegative.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: The crucial step in proving Theorem \ref{thm1 is solving an extremum problem introduced in Proposition \ref{pro 3.4 .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Upon examination of the proof, it becomes evident that for any $\theta$, strictly greater than $\theta(n)$, Proposition \ref{pro 3.4 does not hold.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Consequently, by employing our method, $\theta(n)$ as provided in equation \eqref{1.2 is deemed optimal.}
elements.sent-2411-026: But it remains an interesting question that whether $\theta(n)$ is sharp or not for Theorem \ref{thm1 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: The subsequent sections of this paper are structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-028: In Section \ref{sec 2 , we give the definition of the curvature operator of the second kind and derive some identities for the curvatures on Einstein manifolds.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Section \ref{sec 3 is devoted to the proof of Theorem \ref{thm1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-030: In this section, we set up some notations and recall some basic facts about the curvature operator of the second kind, and refer to \cite{CV60, BK78, OT79, Nishikawa86, Kashiwada93,Li21 for more details.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Let $(M^n, g)$ be an $n$-dimensional manifold, $V=T_p M$, $\displaystyle \{e_i\}_{i=1 ^n$ be an orthonormal basis of $V$ with respect to $g$, and $S^2(V)$ be the space of symmetric two tensors on $V$.}
elements.sent-2411-032: In this paper, we always identify $V$ with its dual space $V^*$ via the metric $g$.
elements.sent-2411-033: Then $S^2(V)$ splits into $O(V)$-irreducible subspaces as \begin{equation}S^2(V)=S^2_0(V)\oplus \R g,\end{equation} where $S^2_0(V)$ is the space of traceless part of $S^2(V)$ and $g=\sum\limits_{i=1 ^{n e_i\otimes e_i$.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: We denote by $N$ the dimension of $S^2_0(V)$, that is \begin{equation}N=\dim(S^2_0(V))=\tfrac{(n-1)(n+2)}{2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-035: The space of symmetric two-tensors on $\wedge^2V$, denoted by $S^2(\wedge^2 V)$, has an orthogonal decomposition \begin{equation}S^2(\wedge^2 V) =S^2_B(\wedge^2 V) \oplus \wedge^4 V,\end{equation} where $S^2_B(\wedge^2 V)$ is the space of algebraic curvature operators on $V$, consisting of all tensors $R\in S^2(\wedge^2V)$ satisfying the first Bianchi identity.
elements.sent-2411-036: The tensor product $\otimes$ is defined by \begin{equation}(e_i\otimes e_j)(e_k,e_l)=\delta_{ik}\delta_{jl},\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\delta_{ij}= 1, i=j, 0, i\neq j. \end{equation} For $e_i$ and $e_j$ in $V$, the symmetric product $\odot$ and wedge product $\wedge$ are defined by \begin{equation}e_i \odot e_j=e_i\otimes e_j +e_j \otimes e_i,\end{equation} and \begin{equation}e_i \wedge e_j=e_i\otimes e_j - e_j \otimes e_i,\end{equation} respectively.
elements.sent-2411-037: Furthermore the inner product on $\wedge^2(V)$ is defined by \begin{equation}\langle A, B \rangle =\frac{1}{2}\tr(A^T B),\end{equation} and the inner product on $S^2(V)$ is defined by \begin{equation}\langle A, B \rangle =\tr(A^T B).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-038: Clearly, $\{e_i \wedge e_j\}_{1\leq i<j\leq n}$ forms an orthonormal basis of $\wedge^2(V)$ and $\{\dfrac{1 {\sqrt{2 e_i \odot e_j\}_{1\leq i<j\leq n \cup \{\dfrac{1 {2 e_i \odot e_i\}_{1\leq i\leq n}$ forms an orthonormal basis of $S^2(V)$.}}}}}}
elements.subsection-2411-002: Curvature Operator of the Second Kind
elements.sent-2411-039: For an algebraic curvature tensor $R\in S^2_B(\wedge^2(T_p M))$, $R$ can induce two self-adjoint operators (see \cite{Nishikawa86 ).}
elements.sent-2411-040: The first one denoted by $\hat{R :\wedge^2 (T_p M) \to \wedge^2(T_p M)$ is defined as \begin{equation}\hat{R}(\omega)_{ij}=\frac{1}{2}\sum_{k,l=1}^n R_{ijkl}\omega_{kl},\end{equation} which is called the curvature operator of the first kind.}
elements.sent-2411-041: The other one, $\overline{R :S^2(T_p M) \to S^2(T_p M)$, is defined by \begin{equation}\overline{R}(\vp)_{ij}=\sum_{k,l=1}^n R_{iklj}\vp_{kl}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-042: The curvature operator of the second kind, introduced by Nishikawa \cite{Nishikawa86 , is symmetric bilinear form \begin{equation}\mathring{R}: S^2_0(T_pM) \times S^2_0(T_pM) \to \R\end{equation} obtained by restricting $\overline{R}$ to $S^2_0(T_pM)$, the space of traceless symmetric two-tensors.}
elements.sent-2411-043: It was pointed out in \cite{NPW22 that the curvature operator of the second kind $\mathring{R}$ can also be interpreted as the self-adjoint operator \begin{equation}\label{2.1} \mathring{R} = \pi \circ \overline{R}: S^2_0(T_pM) \to S^2_0(T_pM),\end{equation} where $\pi: S^2(T_pM) \to S^2_0(T_pM)$ is the projection map.}
elements.sent-2411-044: We denote by $\{\l_j\}_{j=1 ^N$ the eigenvalues of $\mathring{R}$, by $ \bar{\l =\sum_{j=1 ^N \l_j/N $ be the average of the eigenvalues of $\mathring{R}$, by $R$ the Riemann curvature tensor, and by $\S$ be the scalar curvature.}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: For Einstein manifolds, one can obtain the scalar curvature via $\mathring{R}$.. Precisely.
elements.sent-2411-046: Suppose $(M^n,g)$ is an Einstein manifold of dimension $n$.. Then \begin{equation}\label{2.2} \S=n(n-1)\bar\lambda.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-047: Let $\{e_i\}_{i=1 ^n$ be an orthonormal basis of $T_p M$, then $\{\dfrac{1 {\sqrt{2 e_i \odot e_j\}_{1\leq i<j\leq n \cup \{\dfrac{1 {2 e_i \odot e_i\}_{1\leq i\leq n}$ is an orthonormal basis of $S^2(T_pM)$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Since \begin{equation}\left\langle {\bar{R}(\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}{e_i} \odot {e_j}),\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}{e_i} \odot {e_j}} \right\rangle =R_{ijij},\end{equation} then \begin{equation}\operatorname{tr}(\bar{R}) = \sum\limits_{1\le i<j\le n} {\left\langle {\bar{R}(\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}{e_i} \odot {e_j}),\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}{e_i} \odot {e_j}} \right\rangle } + \sum\limits_{1\le i\le n} {\left\langle {\bar{R}(\frac{1}{2}{e_i} \odot {e_i}),\frac{1}{2}{e_i} \odot {e_i}} \right\rangle } = \sum\limits_{1\le i<j\le n} R_{ijij} = \frac{\S}{2}.\end{equation}. Thus, \begin{equation}\operatorname{tr}(\mathring{R})=\operatorname{tr}(\bar{R})-\langle\bar{R}(\frac{g}{\sqrt n}), \frac{g}{\sqrt n}\rangle=\frac{\S}{2}+\frac{\S}{n}=\frac{n+2}{2n}\S.\end{equation}.

elements.subsection-2411-003: A formula for Weyl tensor on Einstein manifolds
elements.sent-2411-053: Given $A, B\in S^2(V)$, their Kulkarni-Nomizu product gives rise to $A \owedge B \in S^2_B(\wedge^2 V)$ via \begin{equation}(A \owedge B )_{ijkl} =A_{ik}B_{jl}+A_{jl}B_{ik} -A_{jk}B_{il}-A_{il}B_{jk}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-057: We recall some curvature notations on $(M^n, g)$: $R$ represents the Riemann curvature operator, $W$ represents the Weyl tensor, $\mathring{R}$ represents the curvature operator of the second kind, $\l_1\le\l_2\le \cdots\le \l_N$ are the eigenvalues of $\mathring{R}$, and $\bar\l=\sum_{j=1 ^N \l_j/N$ is the average of $\l_j$ .}
elements.sent-2411-058: To prove the main theorem, we conduct several computations involving the Riemann curvature through the curvature operator of the second kind on Einstein manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-059: Let $(M^n,g)$ be an $n$ dimensional Einstein manifold.
elements.sent-2411-061: Recall from the equation \eqref{2.4 that \begin{equation}R= \frac{\S}{2n(n-1)}g \owedge g +W,\end{equation} then \begin{equation}|R|^2 = \frac{\S^2}{4n^2(n-1)^2}|g \owedge g|^2 +|W|^2 =\frac{\S^2}{2n(n-1)} +|W|^2,\end{equation} where we used the identity $|g \owedge g|^2=8n(n-1)$ (cf.. \cite[Lemma 7.22]{Lee18 ).}}

elements.sent-2411-065: Suppose the smallest eigenvalue of $\mathring{R}$ satisfies $\lambda_1 \ge -\theta \bar \lambda$, then \begin{equation}\label{3.3} \sum_{j=1}^N \lambda_{j} |S^{j} W|^2 \ge -\frac{8(n^2+n-8)}{3n}\theta \bar\lambda \sum_{j=1}^N \lambda_j^2 +\frac{4(n^2+n-8)(n-1)(n+2)}{3n} \theta \bar\lambda^3,\end{equation} where $S^{j W$ is given by Definition \ref{def2.4 .}}

elements.sent-2411-068: Suppose $(M^n,g)$ is an Einstein manifold satisfying $\lambda_1 \ge -\theta \bar \lambda$.

elements.sent-2411-071: By \eqref{3.3 and $N=(n-1)(n+2)/2$, we derive that \begin{equation}3\langle \Delta R,R\rangle \ge -\frac{8(n^2+n-8)\theta}{3n} \bar\lambda \sum_{j=1}^N \lambda_j^2 +\frac{4(n^2+n-8)(n-1)(n+2)}{3n} \theta \bar\lambda^3+ 16\sum_{j=1}^N {\lambda _j ^3} +\frac{8(n-1)}{3n} \lf(-n^3+6n^2+12n-8 \ri){\bar\lambda^3} +8\lf(\frac{2n^2-22n+8}{3n} \ri) \bar\lambda \sum_{j=1}^N \lambda _j ^2 =\quad \frac{16}{3n}\Big[ N(N-3)\theta-(2N-9n+6)N \Big]\bar\l^3 + \frac{16}{3n} \Big[ (2N-12n+6)-(N-3)\theta \Big] \bar\lambda \sum_{j=1} ^{N}\lambda_{j}^2+16 \sum_{j=1}^{N}\lambda_{j}^3,\end{equation} proving \eqref{3.5 .}}

elements.sent-2411-077: From the expression of $f(\l)$ we can deduce that \begin{equation}\frac{1}{\bar\l^3} f(\l)=C( n, t)+3n F(x),\end{equation} where \begin{equation}C(n, t)= N\big[ (N-3)t-(3N-9n+3) \big] +3nN(t-1)^2(2t+1) + \big[3(N-4n+1)-(N-3)t\big]\big(-2Nt(t-1)+N(t-1)^2\big),\end{equation} and \begin{equation}F(x)=-(3-\frac{N-2}{N-1})t\sum_{j=1}^N x_{j}^2+\sum_{j=1}^N x_{j}^3.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-081: In $\Omega_k$, using the method of Lagrange multipliers, we find that the critical points \begin{equation}x=\big( \underbrace{0, \cdots ,0}_{N-k}, x_{N-k+1}, \cdots, x_N \big)\end{equation} of $\mathcal{L (x,\mu)$ satisfy \begin{equation} 3 x_i^2-2(3-\frac{N-2}{N-1}) t x_i + \mu=0, \sum_{i=N-k+1}^N x_i=Nt, \end{equation} hence the possible critical points are given by \begin{equation}P_{k, l}:= \big( { \underbrace {0, \cdots ,0}_{N-k}, \underbrace {a, \cdots ,a}_{l}, \underbrace {b, \cdots ,b}_{k - l} } \big),\end{equation} for $0\le l\le \frac{k 2$, where $a$ and $b$ are defined through \begin{equation}\label{3.7}  a+b=2(1-\frac{N-2}{3(N-1)})t, la+(k-l)b=Nt. \end{equation} When $l$ equals $\frac k 2$ and equation \eqref{3.7 is solvable, we have $a+b=\frac{2N {k t$ and $1-\frac{N-2 {3(N-1) =\frac N k$, and in this case direct calculation reveals that \begin{equation}\label{3.8} F\big( { \underbrace {0, \cdots ,0}_{N-k}, \underbrace {a, \cdots ,a}_{k/2}, \underbrace {b, \cdots ,b}_{k/2} } \big)=F(P_{k, 0})\end{equation} for $\forall a, b\ge 0$ satisfying $a+b=2Nt/k$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: When $l<\frac k 2$, by solving \eqref{3.7 we derive \begin{equation}a=(A-B)t, \quad b=(A+B)t,\end{equation} where $A=1-\frac{N-2 {3(N-1)}$, $B=\frac{N-Ak {k-2l}$, and direct calculation gives \begin{equation}F(P_{k,l}) =t^3 \lf(-2kA^3-3A^2(N-Ak)+\frac{(N-Ak)^3}{(k-2l)^2} \ri),\end{equation} hence \begin{equation}\label{3.9} F(P_{k, 0})<F(P_{k, l}), \quad 1\le l<k/2.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: Therefore we conclude from \eqref{3.8 and \eqref{3.9 that \begin{equation}\min\Big\{F(x): \sum_{j=1}^n x_j=N t, x_j\ge 0\Big\}=\min\Big\{F(P_{k, 0}), k=1,2,\cdots, N\Big\},\end{equation} which indicates that the minimum points of the function $f(\l)$, subject to the constraint $\l_i\ge -\theta \bar \l$, are included among \begin{equation}\l^m=( \underbrace{-\theta\bar{\l},\cdots,-\theta\bar{\l}}_{m}, \underbrace {\frac{N+m\theta}{N-m}\bar{\l},\cdots, \frac{N+m\theta}{N-m}\bar{\l}}_{N-m} )\end{equation} for $m=0, 1, \cdots, N-1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-084: For $\l=\l^m$, we calculate that \begin{equation}\frac{1}{\bar\l^2}\sum_{j=1}^{N} \lambda^2_{j} =m \theta^2+(N-m)\lf(\frac{N+m \theta}{N-m}\ri)^2 =N+\frac{N}{N-m}mt^2,\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\frac{1}{\bar\l^3}\sum_{j=1}^{N} \lambda^3_{j} =-m t^3 \frac{(N-2m)N}{(N-m)^2}+\frac{3mN}{N-m}t^2+N,\end{equation} we then conclude \begin{equation}\label{3.10} \frac{1}{\bar\l^3}f(\l^m)= \Big[-\frac{N(N-3)m}{N-m}-\frac{3nmN(N-2m)}{(N-m)^2}\Big]t^3+\frac{3N(N+1-n)}{N-m}mt^2 = \frac{mNt^2}{N-m} \Big[3(N+1-n)-(N-3+\frac{3n(N-2m)}{N-m})t\Big].\nonumber\end{equation} From the definition of $\theta$ in \eqref{1.2 , we see \begin{equation}t=1+\theta=\frac{3(N+1-n)}{N-3+3n(N-2)/(N-1)},\end{equation} and substituting above to \eqref{3.10 , we find \begin{equation}f(\l^0)=f(\l^1)=0, \quad and\quad f(\l^k)>f(\l^1)=0 \quad (k=2,3, \cdots, N-1).\end{equation}.}}

elements.sent-2411-089: Besides, Poincar\'e duality shows that rational cohomology is determined by rational homology \cite[Chapter 3.3]{Hatcher02 .}
elements.sent-2411-090: Thus we conclude that $M$ is either flat or a symmetric space which is also a rational cohomology sphere.
elements.sent-2411-091: Compact symmetric spaces which are real cohomology spheres were classified by Wolf in \cite[Theorem 1]{Wolf .}
elements.sent-2411-092: If $\bar{\l >0$, apart from spheres, $SU(3)/SO(3)$ is the only simply connected example.}
elements.sent-2411-093: Proposition \ref{pro 3.4 implies that the eigenvalues of $\mathring{R}$ is either $\lambda=(1,\cdots,1)\bar{\l}$ or $\lambda=(-\theta, \frac{N+\theta {N-1 ,\cdots, \frac{N+\theta {N-1 )\bar{\l}$, and combining with \cite[Example 4.5]{NPW22 , we know that $M$ is a round sphere.}}}}}}
